root_cause: Need consistent error handling pattern for server actions that works with TypeScript
fix: Define action result type: type ActionResult<T> = { success: true; data: T } | { success: false; error: string; fieldErrors?: Record<string, string[]> }

Server action: 'use server'; export async function createPost(formData: FormData): Promise<ActionResult<Post>> { const parsed = PostSchema.safeParse(Object.fromEntries(formData)); if (!parsed.success) return { success: false, error: 'Validation failed', fieldErrors: parsed.error.flatten().fieldErrors }; try { const post = await prisma.post.create({ data: parsed.data }); revalidatePath('/posts'); return { success: true, data: post }; } catch (e) { return { success: false, error: 'Failed to create post' } } }
Client: use useActionState (React 19) or useFormState for form state management

Display field errors: fieldErrors?.title?.map(err =>

{err}

)

root_cause: Need to invalidate related cache entries when data changes without over-invalidating
fix: Tag-based invalidation: store key-to-tag mapping in Redis sets
On write: await redis.sadd('tag:user:123', 'profile:123', 'posts:user:123', 'feed:latest'); await redis.set('profile:123', data, { ex: 3600 })
On invalidation: const keys = await redis.smembers('tag:user:123'); if (keys.length) await redis.del(...keys); await redis.del('tag:user:123')

Pattern-based: use key prefixes and scan (expensive, use sparingly): const keys = []; let cursor = '0'; do { const [next, found] = await redis.scan(cursor, { match: 'user:123:*', count: 100 }); cursor = next; keys.push(...found) } while (cursor !== '0')
Time-based: just set appropriate TTL and let data expire naturally (simplest approach)
Event-driven: emit event on write, invalidation handler subscribes and clears relevant caches

root_cause: Need comprehensive error monitoring and observability for production Next.js app
fix: Install Sentry: npx @sentry/wizard@latest -i nextjs
Configure: sentry.client.config.ts, sentry.server.config.ts, sentry.edge.config.ts
Wrap next.config.js: const { withSentryConfig } = require('@sentry/nextjs'); module.exports = withSentryConfig(nextConfig, sentryOptions)
Track server action errors: Sentry.captureException(error) in catch blocks
Use Vercel's built-in: Vercel Web Analytics + Speed Insights for core metrics
For Redis/DB monitoring: Upstash Console metrics, Neon Dashboard, Supabase Dashboard
Custom logging: use structured logging (pino) and forward to log drain

root_cause: Need components to respond to their container size rather than viewport size
fix: Enable plugin: npm install @tailwindcss/container-queries (or built-in in Tailwind v3.4+)
Mark container: className='@container'
Respond to container: className='@md:grid-cols-2 @lg:grid-cols-3'
Named containers: className='@container/sidebar' → child: '@md/sidebar:block'
Custom sizes in config: theme: { containers: { xs: '320px', sm: '480px' } }
Use for reusable components that might appear in different layout contexts (sidebar vs main)

root_cause: SQLite has limited ALTER TABLE support - cannot modify/drop columns directly
fix: SQLite only supports ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN and RENAME COLUMN
For schema changes requiring column drops/modifications, Drizzle uses table recreation strategy
Use drizzle-kit push:sqlite for development (destructive but fast)
For production migrations: drizzle-kit generate:sqlite creates proper migration with table recreation
Manual migration pattern: CREATE new table → INSERT INTO new SELECT FROM old → DROP old → RENAME new
Test migrations on local SQLite before running against Turso
Backup Turso before risky migrations: turso db dump your-db > backup.sql

symptoms: implement API response caching with Redis
root_cause: Need to cache external API responses to reduce costs, latency, and rate limit consumption
fix: Create cache wrapper: async function cachedFetch<T>(url: string, options: { ttl?: number; tags?: string[] } = {}): Promise<T> { const cacheKey = `api:${crypto.createHash('sha256').update(url).digest('hex')}`; const cached = await redis.get<T>(cacheKey); if (cached) return cached; const response = await fetch(url); const data = await response.json(); await redis.set(cacheKey, data, { ex: options.ttl ?? 300 }); if (options.tags) { for (const tag of options.tags) await redis.sadd(`tag:${tag}`, cacheKey) } return data as T; }
Usage: const weather = await cachedFetch<WeatherData>('https://api.weather.com/current', { ttl: 600, tags: ['weather'] })
Invalidate: async function invalidateTag(tag: string) { const keys = await redis.smembers(`tag:${tag}`); if (keys.length) await redis.del(...keys, `tag:${tag}`) }
Add stale-while-revalidate: store timestamp, serve stale while refreshing in background

root_cause: Need architecture that provides type safety from database schema to client components
fix: Layer 1 - Database: Prisma schema → generates TypeScript types, or Drizzle schema → inferred types
Layer 2 - Validation: Zod schemas share types with Prisma: const CreatePostSchema = z.object({...}); type CreatePost = z.infer<typeof CreatePostSchema>
Layer 3 - Server: Server Actions/API routes use Prisma types + Zod validation
Layer 4 - Client: Import shared types from lib/types.ts, use with React Hook Form + zodResolver
Layer 5 - Response: Define ActionResult<T> types for consistent server-client contract
Generate types from external sources: Supabase gen types, OpenAPI → zod codegen
Single source of truth: Prisma schema + Zod schemas = all types you need

symptoms: Supabase auth slow on first load
root_cause: Auth session validation adding latency to every request through middleware
fix: Cache auth state client-side: Supabase SDK handles this automatically with localStorage
Minimize middleware auth checks: only validate on protected routes, not static assets
Use lightweight session check in middleware: supabase.auth.getUser() (validates JWT without DB call on every request)
Preload session in root layout: const { data: { session } } = await supabase.auth.getSession()
Pass session to client via context to avoid waterfall: <SessionProvider session={session}>
For static pages: check auth client-side with redirect, not in middleware

symptoms: how to store files locally in development
filesystem storage local vs blob production
root_cause: Need file storage that uses local filesystem in development but Vercel Blob in production (no persistent filesystem on Vercel)
fix: Create storage abstraction: lib/storage.ts with interface: upload(file, path), getUrl(path), delete(path)
Local implementation: use fs.writeFile to /tmp or ./uploads directory
Production implementation: use @vercel/blob for Vercel Blob Storage
Environment switch: export const storage = process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development' ? localStorage : blobStorage
Add uploads/ to .gitignore for local development files
For local: serve files via /api/files/[...path] route that reads from filesystem

root_cause: Need to implement file uploads using Vercel Blob Storage for production deployments
fix: Install: npm install @vercel/blob
Server-side upload: import { put } from '@vercel/blob'; const blob = await put(`uploads/${filename}`, file, { access: 'public', addRandomSuffix: true }); // returns { url, pathname }
Client upload (large files): import { upload } from '@vercel/blob/client'; const blob = await upload(filename, file, { access: 'public', handleUploadUrl: '/api/upload' })

Create upload handler: app/api/upload/route.ts: import { handleUpload } from '@vercel/blob'; export async function POST(req) { const body = await handleUpload({ request: req, onBeforeGenerateToken: async (pathname) => ({ allowedContentTypes: ['image/*'] }) }); return Response.json(body) }
Delete: import { del } from '@vercel/blob'; await del(blobUrl)
Set BLOB_READ_WRITE_TOKEN in Vercel environment variables (auto-set with Vercel Blob addon)

symptoms: save files to disk in development
root_cause: Need local file storage that mimics blob storage behavior for development without cloud dependency
fix: Create local storage helper: import { writeFile, mkdir, unlink } from 'fs/promises'; import path from 'path'

Upload: async function localUpload(file: Buffer, filename: string) { const dir = path.join(process.cwd(), 'uploads'); await mkdir(dir, { recursive: true }); const filepath = path.join(dir, filename); await writeFile(filepath, file); return { url: `/api/files/${filename}`, pathname: filename } }

Serve files route: app/api/files/[...path]/route.ts: import { readFile } from 'fs/promises'; export async function GET(req, { params }) { const filepath = path.join(process.cwd(), 'uploads', params.path.join('/')); const file = await readFile(filepath); return new Response(file, { headers: { 'Content-Type': getMimeType(filepath) } }) }
Delete: async function localDelete(filename: string) { await unlink(path.join(process.cwd(), 'uploads', filename)) }
Add to .gitignore: uploads/
Note: /tmp is also available but cleared between restarts

symptoms: swap storage provider based on environment
root_cause: Need a single interface that transparently uses filesystem locally and Vercel Blob in production
fix: Define interface: interface StorageProvider { upload(file: Buffer | File, path: string): Promise<{ url: string; pathname: string }>; getUrl(pathname: string): string; delete(pathname: string): Promise<void>; }

Local provider: class LocalStorage implements StorageProvider { async upload(file, path) { await writeFile(`./uploads/${path}`, file instanceof File ? Buffer.from(await file.arrayBuffer()) : file); return { url: `/api/files/${path}`, pathname: path } } ... }

Blob provider: class BlobStorage implements StorageProvider { async upload(file, path) { const blob = await put(path, file, { access: 'public' }); return { url: blob.url, pathname: blob.pathname } } ... }
Export singleton: export const storage: StorageProvider = process.env.VERCEL ? new BlobStorage() : new LocalStorage()
Usage: const { url } = await storage.upload(fileBuffer, `avatars/${userId}.png`)

root_cause: Vercel Blob token not configured or client upload handler missing proper authentication
fix: Ensure BLOB_READ_WRITE_TOKEN is set in Vercel environment variables
For Vercel Blob addon: token is auto-provisioned - check Vercel Dashboard > Storage
For client uploads: handleUpload route must exist and validate the request
Check file size limits: Vercel Blob supports up to 500MB per file (Pro plan)
Verify access parameter: 'public' for publicly accessible files
For local development: BLOB_READ_WRITE_TOKEN must be in .env.local (get from Vercel Dashboard > Storage > Tokens)
Common error: passing wrong content type - let the SDK detect it or set explicitly

symptoms: local uploads not persisting on Vercel
root_cause: Vercel serverless functions have ephemeral filesystem - files written to disk are lost between invocations
fix: NEVER use filesystem for persistent storage on Vercel - use Vercel Blob, S3, or Cloudflare R2
/tmp is available but limited to 512MB and cleared between cold starts
For caching: use /tmp for temporary computation files, Redis for persistent cache
Migration: replace all fs.writeFile production usage with Vercel Blob put()
Environment check: if (process.env.VERCEL) use blob storage else use local filesystem
Database files (SQLite): NOT possible on Vercel filesystem - use Turso/Neon instead

symptoms: how to setup Google OAuth in Next.js
root_cause: Need to implement Google OAuth authentication for user login in Next.js application
fix: Install: npm install next-auth @auth/core

Create auth config: lib/auth.ts with GoogleProvider: import GoogleProvider from 'next-auth/providers/google'; export const authOptions = { providers: [GoogleProvider({ clientId: process.env.GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID!, clientSecret: process.env.GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET! })] }

Create API route: app/api/auth/[...nextauth]/route.ts: import NextAuth from 'next-auth'; import { authOptions } from '@/lib/auth'; const handler = NextAuth(authOptions); export { handler as GET, handler as POST }
Get credentials: Google Cloud Console > APIs & Services > Credentials > Create OAuth 2.0 Client ID
Set authorized redirect URI: https://your-domain.com/api/auth/callback/google
For local dev: add http://localhost:3000/api/auth/callback/google as redirect URI
Set env vars: GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID, GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET, NEXTAUTH_SECRET, NEXTAUTH_URL

root_cause: The redirect URI in Google Cloud Console doesn't exactly match the callback URL used by NextAuth
fix: Authorized redirect URI must be EXACTLY: https://your-domain.com/api/auth/callback/google
For Vercel preview deployments: add https://*.vercel.app/api/auth/callback/google (wildcard not supported - use specific preview URL)
For local development: add http://localhost:3000/api/auth/callback/google
Check for trailing slashes - they must match exactly
In Google Cloud Console: APIs & Services > Credentials > Your OAuth Client > Authorized redirect URIs
Changes in Google Console can take a few minutes to propagate
Set NEXTAUTH_URL to match your production domain exactly (with https://)

symptoms: request Google API access during login
OAuth scopes for Calendar and Sheets
root_cause: Need to request additional Google API permissions beyond basic profile during OAuth flow
fix: Add scopes to GoogleProvider: GoogleProvider({ clientId, clientSecret, authorization: { params: { scope: 'openid email profile https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar https://www.googleapis.com/auth/spreadsheets', access_type: 'offline', prompt: 'consent' } } })

Store tokens for API access: callbacks: { async jwt({ token, account }) { if (account) { token.accessToken = account.access_token; token.refreshToken = account.refresh_token; token.expiresAt = account.expires_at } return token } }
Pass to session: callbacks: { async session({ session, token }) { session.accessToken = token.accessToken; return session } }
access_type: 'offline' gives you a refresh_token for long-lived access
prompt: 'consent' forces consent screen to get refresh_token on re-auth
Store refresh_token in database for background API access

symptoms: 401 from Google API after some time
root_cause: Google access tokens expire after 1 hour; need to implement token refresh using refresh_token
fix: Implement token refresh in JWT callback: async jwt({ token }) { if (Date.now() < token.expiresAt * 1000) return token; return await refreshGoogleToken(token) }

Refresh function: async function refreshGoogleToken(token) { const response = await fetch('https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token', { method: 'POST', body: new URLSearchParams({ client_id: process.env.GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID!, client_secret: process.env.GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET!, grant_type: 'refresh_token', refresh_token: token.refreshToken }) }); const data = await response.json(); return { ...token, accessToken: data.access_token, expiresAt: Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000) + data.expires_in } }
Must have requested access_type: 'offline' during initial consent to get refresh_token
Store refresh_token in DB: it doesn't change and is needed for background jobs
If refresh fails: redirect user to re-authenticate

symptoms: Google OAuth consent screen not verified
root_cause: App using sensitive/restricted scopes requires Google verification before production use
fix: For development/testing: add test users in Google Cloud Console > OAuth consent screen > Test users
Only test users can access unverified apps with sensitive scopes
For production: submit for Google verification (can take weeks)
Scopes requiring verification: calendar, sheets, drive, gmail (any data access scope)
Basic scopes (no verification needed): openid, email, profile
Publishing status: set to 'In production' only after verification for sensitive scopes
Alternative: use service accounts for server-to-server access (no user consent needed)

symptoms: store Google OAuth tokens in database
root_cause: Need to persist OAuth tokens for background API access beyond the user's active session
fix: Use Prisma adapter: npm install @auth/prisma-adapter
Configure: import { PrismaAdapter } from '@auth/prisma-adapter'; export const authOptions = { adapter: PrismaAdapter(prisma), ... }
Add Account model to schema.prisma with: access_token, refresh_token, expires_at, token_type, scope fields
Retrieve tokens for background use: const account = await prisma.account.findFirst({ where: { userId, provider: 'google' } })
Token refresh in background: use the stored refresh_token to get new access_token for cron jobs or Inngest functions
Encrypt sensitive tokens at rest: use crypto.createCipheriv before storing

symptoms: how to setup Google Calendar API in Next.js
root_cause: Need to read/write Google Calendar events using the Google Calendar API with OAuth tokens
fix: Install: npm install googleapis
Create Calendar client: import { google } from 'googleapis'; const calendar = google.calendar({ version: 'v3', headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${accessToken}` } })

Or with OAuth2 client: const auth = new google.auth.OAuth2(clientId, clientSecret); auth.setCredentials({ access_token: accessToken, refresh_token: refreshToken }); const calendar = google.calendar({ version: 'v3', auth })
Required scope: https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar (read/write) or https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar.readonly
Enable API: Google Cloud Console > APIs & Services > Enable Google Calendar API
List events: const { data } = await calendar.events.list({ calendarId: 'primary', timeMin: new Date().toISOString(), maxResults: 10, singleEvents: true, orderBy: 'startTime' })

root_cause: Need to create calendar events on behalf of authenticated users
fix: Create event: const event = await calendar.events.insert({ calendarId: 'primary', requestBody: { summary: 'Meeting', description: 'Team sync', start: { dateTime: '2024-01-15T09:00:00', timeZone: 'America/New_York' }, end: { dateTime: '2024-01-15T10:00:00', timeZone: 'America/New_York' }, attendees: [{ email: 'colleague@example.com' }], reminders: { useDefault: false, overrides: [{ method: 'popup', minutes: 10 }] } } })
With conference (Google Meet): add conferenceData: { createRequest: { requestId: uuid() } } and conferenceDataVersion: 1 in params
Recurring event: add recurrence: ['RRULE:FREQ=WEEKLY;COUNT=10']
All-day event: use date instead of dateTime: start: { date: '2024-01-15' }
Returns event with id, htmlLink (URL to event), and hangoutLink (if Meet added)

root_cause: Need to retrieve calendar events for display or processing
fix: List upcoming events: const { data } = await calendar.events.list({ calendarId: 'primary', timeMin: new Date().toISOString(), timeMax: endOfWeek.toISOString(), singleEvents: true, orderBy: 'startTime', maxResults: 50 }); const events = data.items
Search events: await calendar.events.list({ calendarId: 'primary', q: 'search term' })
Get specific event: await calendar.events.get({ calendarId: 'primary', eventId: 'event_id' })
List all calendars: await calendar.calendarList.list() // returns user's calendar list
Paginate: use pageToken from response for next page: if (data.nextPageToken) { await calendar.events.list({ ...params, pageToken: data.nextPageToken }) }
Parse event time: event.start.dateTime (timed) or event.start.date (all-day)

root_cause: OAuth token missing required calendar scope or API not enabled in Google Cloud project
fix: Verify scope includes calendar: check that 'https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar' was requested during OAuth
Enable API: Google Cloud Console > APIs & Services > Library > Google Calendar API > Enable
User must re-consent if new scopes added after initial login: prompt: 'consent' in OAuth config
Check token scopes: decode JWT or check account.scope in NextAuth callback
For read-only: use https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar.readonly scope
If using service account: calendar must be shared with service account email
Revoke and re-authorize: redirect user to /api/auth/signin to get fresh consent

root_cause: Need to receive real-time notifications when calendar events change
fix: Set up watch: await calendar.events.watch({ calendarId: 'primary', requestBody: { id: uniqueChannelId, type: 'web_hook', address: 'https://your-app.vercel.app/api/calendar/webhook', token: 'verification-token', expiration: Date.now() + 7 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000 } })
Create webhook handler: app/api/calendar/webhook/route.ts that processes X-Goog-Resource-State header
On notification: sync events using syncToken from last list() call for incremental sync
Renew watch before expiration (max 7 days): use cron/QStash to renew
Store syncToken in database per user for efficient delta sync
Webhook receives notification of CHANGE, not the actual event data - you must fetch changes

root_cause: Calendar events display wrong time due to timezone handling between server, client, and Google API
fix: Always specify timeZone in event creation: start: { dateTime: '2024-01-15T09:00:00-05:00', timeZone: 'America/New_York' }
Get user timezone: from browser Intl.DateTimeFormat().resolvedOptions().timeZone
List events in user timezone: calendar.events.list({ ...params, timeZone: userTimeZone })
Parse dateTime with timezone: new Date(event.start.dateTime) handles ISO format correctly
For display: use date-fns-tz or Intl.DateTimeFormat for consistent timezone rendering
Store user timezone preference in database for server-side operations
All-day events use date (no timezone): { date: '2024-01-15' } represents the date in any timezone

root_cause: Google Calendar API has per-user and per-project rate limits that can be exceeded with frequent calls
fix: Default quota: 1,000,000 queries/day, but per-user limit is ~100 queries/100 seconds
Cache calendar data in Redis: await redis.set(`cal:${userId}:events`, events, { ex: 300 })
Use incremental sync with syncToken instead of full list on every request
Batch requests: const batch = google.newBatch(); batch.add(calendar.events.list(params1)); batch.add(calendar.events.list(params2)); await batch
Implement exponential backoff on 429 responses
Use webhook push notifications instead of polling for changes
Pre-fetch and cache during user session start, serve from cache during session

symptoms: how to setup Google Sheets API in Next.js
root_cause: Need to read/write Google Sheets data using the Google Sheets API with OAuth or service account
fix: Install: npm install googleapis
Create Sheets client: import { google } from 'googleapis'; const sheets = google.sheets({ version: 'v4', headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${accessToken}` } })

Or with service account (no user OAuth needed): const auth = new google.auth.GoogleAuth({ credentials: JSON.parse(process.env.GOOGLE_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_KEY!), scopes: ['https://www.googleapis.com/auth/spreadsheets'] }); const sheets = google.sheets({ version: 'v4', auth })
Required scope: https://www.googleapis.com/auth/spreadsheets (read/write) or https://www.googleapis.com/auth/spreadsheets.readonly
Enable API: Google Cloud Console > APIs & Services > Enable Google Sheets API
For service account: share the spreadsheet with the service account email address

root_cause: Need to read cell/range data from a Google Spreadsheet
fix: Read range: const { data } = await sheets.spreadsheets.values.get({ spreadsheetId: 'SPREADSHEET_ID', range: 'Sheet1!A1:D10' }); const rows = data.values
Read entire sheet: range: 'Sheet1' (omit cell range)
Multiple ranges: await sheets.spreadsheets.values.batchGet({ spreadsheetId, ranges: ['Sheet1!A:A', 'Sheet2!B:C'] })
Get with formatting: await sheets.spreadsheets.get({ spreadsheetId, ranges: ['Sheet1'], includeGridData: true })
Parse rows to objects: const [headers, ...rows] = data.values; const items = rows.map(row => Object.fromEntries(headers.map((h, i) => [h, row[i] ?? ''])))
Spreadsheet ID: from URL https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/{SPREADSHEET_ID}/edit

root_cause: Need to write, update, or append data to a Google Spreadsheet
fix: Update cells: await sheets.spreadsheets.values.update({ spreadsheetId, range: 'Sheet1!A1:C1', valueInputOption: 'USER_ENTERED', requestBody: { values: [['Name', 'Email', 'Date']] } })

Append rows: await sheets.spreadsheets.values.append({ spreadsheetId, range: 'Sheet1!A:C', valueInputOption: 'USER_ENTERED', insertDataOption: 'INSERT_ROWS', requestBody: { values: [['John', 'john@example.com', new Date().toISOString()]] } })
Batch update: await sheets.spreadsheets.values.batchUpdate({ spreadsheetId, requestBody: { valueInputOption: 'USER_ENTERED', data: [{ range: 'Sheet1!A1', values: [['val']] }] } })
Clear range: await sheets.spreadsheets.values.clear({ spreadsheetId, range: 'Sheet1!A2:Z' })
valueInputOption: 'USER_ENTERED' parses formulas/dates, 'RAW' stores as-is

symptoms: Google Sheets backend for small app
root_cause: Need to use Google Sheets as a lightweight data source or CMS for small applications
fix: Use service account for server-side access (no user login required): share sheet with service account email

Create typed data layer: async function getProducts(): Promise<Product[]> { const { data } = await sheets.spreadsheets.values.get({ spreadsheetId, range: 'Products!A2:E' }); return data.values?.map(([name, price, stock, category, image]) => ({ name, price: Number(price), stock: Number(stock), category, image })) ?? [] }
Cache with Redis: const products = await getCached('products', getProducts, 300)
Revalidate on sheet update: use ISR with revalidate interval or webhook trigger
Limitations: no concurrent writes, 10M cells per spreadsheet, rate limits
Good for: small datasets, content managed by non-developers, prototyping
Not suitable for: high-traffic apps, complex queries, relational data

root_cause: Service account or OAuth user doesn't have access to the specific spreadsheet
fix: For service account: share the spreadsheet with the service account email (found in service account JSON: client_email field)
Share as 'Editor' for read+write, 'Viewer' for read-only
For OAuth users: they can only access their own sheets or sheets shared with them
Verify API is enabled: Google Cloud Console > APIs & Services > Google Sheets API must be enabled
Check scopes: ensure spreadsheets scope was granted during OAuth consent
Common mistake: using wrong spreadsheet ID - extract from URL between /d/ and /edit
For organization accounts: check if domain-wide delegation is needed

symptoms: configure service account for Google APIs
root_cause: Need to configure Google service account credentials for server-to-server API access on Vercel
fix: Create service account: Google Cloud Console > IAM & Admin > Service Accounts > Create
Download JSON key file, extract credentials
Store as env var: GOOGLE_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_KEY (full JSON stringified) or split into GOOGLE_CLIENT_EMAIL and GOOGLE_PRIVATE_KEY

Initialize auth: const auth = new google.auth.GoogleAuth({ credentials: { client_email: process.env.GOOGLE_CLIENT_EMAIL, private_key: process.env.GOOGLE_PRIVATE_KEY?.replace(/\\n/g, '\n') }, scopes: ['https://www.googleapis.com/auth/spreadsheets', 'https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar'] })
IMPORTANT: Vercel env vars escape newlines in private keys - use .replace(/\\n/g, '\n') to fix
Alternative: store JSON key as base64: GOOGLE_CREDENTIALS_BASE64=<base64 string>, decode at runtime
Never commit service account keys to git - use environment variables only

symptoms: Google private key error on Vercel
root_cause: Private key newlines are escaped when stored in Vercel environment variables, breaking PEM format
fix: Fix escaped newlines: const privateKey = process.env.GOOGLE_PRIVATE_KEY?.replace(/\\n/g, '\n')
Or store as base64: encode key with btoa(), decode with Buffer.from(env, 'base64').toString()
In Vercel Dashboard: paste private key with actual newlines (multi-line env var support)
Alternative: store entire service account JSON as single env var and parse: JSON.parse(process.env.GOOGLE_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_JSON!)
Verify key format starts with: -----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY----- and ends with -----END PRIVATE KEY-----
Test locally first: ensure .env.local has the key in correct format

root_cause: Need to minimize API calls to Google Sheets for better performance and quota usage
fix: Batch read: use batchGet for multiple ranges in one call: await sheets.spreadsheets.values.batchGet({ spreadsheetId, ranges: ['Sheet1!A:Z', 'Config!A:B'] })

Batch write: use batchUpdate for multiple updates: await sheets.spreadsheets.values.batchUpdate({ spreadsheetId, requestBody: { valueInputOption: 'USER_ENTERED', data: [{range: 'A1', values: [['a']]}, {range: 'B1', values: [['b']]}] } })
Cache reads in Redis: const data = await getCached(`sheet:${spreadsheetId}`, () => fetchSheet(), 60)
Use append instead of calculating next row: append auto-finds the next empty row
For bulk operations: prepare all data in memory, write in single batch call
Rate limits: 60 reads/min/user, 60 writes/min/user - batch to stay within limits

root_cause: Need to create new spreadsheets and populate them with data for reporting or export
fix: Create spreadsheet: const { data: spreadsheet } = await sheets.spreadsheets.create({ requestBody: { properties: { title: 'Monthly Report - Jan 2024' }, sheets: [{ properties: { title: 'Summary' } }, { properties: { title: 'Details' } }] } })
Get new spreadsheet ID: const spreadsheetId = spreadsheet.spreadsheetId

Populate with data: await sheets.spreadsheets.values.batchUpdate({ spreadsheetId, requestBody: { valueInputOption: 'USER_ENTERED', data: [{ range: 'Summary!A1', values: [['Metric', 'Value'], ['Revenue', '$10,000']] }] } })
Share with user: use Google Drive API: await drive.permissions.create({ fileId: spreadsheetId, requestBody: { type: 'user', role: 'writer', emailAddress: userEmail } })
Return link: `https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/${spreadsheetId}/edit`
Format cells: use spreadsheets.batchUpdate with UpdateCellsRequest for formatting

symptoms: initialize Google APIs with token refresh
root_cause: Need a reusable utility to create authenticated Google API clients with automatic token refresh
fix: Create lib/google.ts: import { google } from 'googleapis'; export async function getGoogleClient(userId: string) { const account = await prisma.account.findFirst({ where: { userId, provider: 'google' } }); if (!account) throw new Error('Google account not linked'); const auth = new google.auth.OAuth2(process.env.GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID, process.env.GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET); auth.setCredentials({ access_token: account.access_token, refresh_token: account.refresh_token, expiry_date: account.expires_at ? account.expires_at * 1000 : undefined }); auth.on('tokens', async (tokens) => { await prisma.account.update({ where: { id: account.id }, data: { access_token: tokens.access_token, expires_at: tokens.expiry_date ? Math.floor(tokens.expiry_date / 1000) : null } }) }); return { calendar: google.calendar({ version: 'v3', auth }), sheets: google.sheets({ version: 'v4', auth }), drive: google.drive({ version: 'v3', auth }) } }
Usage: const { calendar, sheets } = await getGoogleClient(session.user.id)
Auto-refreshes tokens and persists new access_token to database
Handles token expiration transparently for all API calls

root_cause: Clear project structure improves maintainability, onboarding speed, and code discoverability
fix: Use src/ directory: src/app/, src/lib/, src/components/, src/hooks/, src/types/
Colocate page-specific components in the route folder: app/dashboard/_components/
Shared UI components in src/components/ui/ (buttons, inputs, modals)
Business logic in src/lib/: src/lib/db.ts, src/lib/auth.ts, src/lib/redis.ts
Server-only code in src/lib/server/ with 'import server-only' guard
Type definitions in src/types/ or colocated as component-name.types.ts

symptoms: server component vs client component decision
root_cause: Minimizing client components reduces bundle size and improves performance
fix: Default to Server Components - only add 'use client' when you need interactivity
Push 'use client' boundary as far down the tree as possible
Pattern: Server Component wrapper fetches data, passes to thin Client Component for interactivity
Client components needed for: useState, useEffect, event handlers, browser APIs, context providers
Server components for: data fetching, database access, heavy computation, sensitive logic
Never import server-only modules in client components - use the 'server-only' package guard

root_cause: Consistent API design improves maintainability and client integration
fix: Use HTTP methods correctly: GET (read), POST (create), PUT (full update), PATCH (partial), DELETE
Return consistent response shape: { data, error, meta } across all endpoints
Always validate request body with Zod before processing
Return appropriate status codes: 200 (ok), 201 (created), 400 (bad input), 401 (unauthorized), 404, 500
Use route groups for API versioning: app/api/v1/users/route.ts
Add proper error handling: try/catch with typed error responses

symptoms: metadata and SEO best practice Next.js
root_cause: Proper metadata configuration improves SEO rankings and social sharing
fix: Export metadata object or generateMetadata function from page.tsx and layout.tsx
Static: export const metadata: Metadata = { title: 'Page', description: '...' }
Dynamic: export async function generateMetadata({ params }): Promise<Metadata> { const post = await getPost(params.id); return { title: post.title, openGraph: { images: [post.image] } } }
Set template in root layout: title: { template: '%s | MyApp', default: 'MyApp' }
Add OpenGraph images: create app/opengraph-image.tsx for auto-generated OG images
Add sitemap: create app/sitemap.ts returning URL array with lastModified dates

root_cause: Comprehensive error handling prevents white screens and improves user experience
fix: Create app/error.tsx (catches route segment errors), app/global-error.tsx (root errors), app/not-found.tsx (404)
Server Actions: always return { success, data?, error? } - never throw to the client
Use error.tsx 'reset' prop: <button onClick={() => reset()}>Try Again</button>
Log errors server-side (Sentry/logging) before returning user-friendly message
Validate all external inputs (user input, API responses, URL params) with Zod
Implement retry logic for transient failures (network, database timeouts)

root_cause: Proper loading states prevent layout shift and improve perceived performance
fix: Create loading.tsx in route segments for automatic Suspense boundaries
Use skeleton components that match final layout dimensions to prevent CLS
Wrap independent data-fetching components in <Suspense fallback={<Skeleton />}>
For forms: use useFormStatus() to show pending state on submit buttons
Show optimistic updates immediately, revert on error
Use streaming for long data fetches: partial page renders while rest loads

root_cause: Proper environment variable management prevents security leaks and deployment issues
fix: NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix ONLY for values safe to expose in browser bundle
Server-only secrets (API keys, DB URLs): never prefix with NEXT_PUBLIC_
Use .env.local for local dev, .env.example as template (committed, no real values)
Validate env vars at build time: create src/env.ts with zod schema validation
Different values per environment: Vercel Dashboard > Settings > Environment Variables (Production/Preview/Development)
Never log or return env vars in API responses - even accidentally in error messages

root_cause: Well-designed database schema reduces complexity and prevents performance issues at scale
fix: Always add createdAt/updatedAt timestamps: createdAt DateTime @default(now()), updatedAt DateTime @updatedAt
Use UUID for public-facing IDs, auto-increment for internal: id String @id @default(cuid())
Add indexes on columns used in WHERE/ORDER BY: @@index([userId, createdAt])
Use enums for fixed value sets: enum Role { USER ADMIN }
Soft delete pattern: add deletedAt DateTime? field, filter with WHERE deletedAt IS NULL
Separate concerns: User (auth) vs Profile (display) vs Settings (preferences)

root_cause: Effective caching strategy balances freshness, performance, and cost
fix: Level 1 - Browser: Cache-Control headers for static assets (Vercel handles automatically)
Level 2 - CDN/Edge: ISR with revalidate for semi-static pages (product pages, blog posts)
Level 3 - Application: Redis cache for computed data (aggregations, API responses)
Level 4 - Database: Connection pooling + query result caching
Rule: cache at the highest level possible, invalidate at the lowest level necessary
Use cache tags for surgical invalidation: fetch(url, { next: { tags: ['products'] } })

root_cause: Proper security headers protect against XSS, clickjacking, and other client-side attacks
fix: Add security headers in next.config.js or middleware: X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy
Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'unsafe-eval' 'unsafe-inline'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'
Use next.config.js headers: async headers() { return [{ source: '/(.*)', headers: securityHeaders }] }
Set Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains (HSTS)
Permissions-Policy: camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=() to restrict browser features
Vercel automatically sets X-Vercel-Id and handles HTTPS redirect

root_cause: NEXT_PUBLIC_ variables are embedded in the JavaScript bundle and visible to anyone
fix: NEVER use NEXT_PUBLIC_ for: database URLs, API secret keys, OAuth secrets, encryption keys
Check for leaks: search codebase for NEXT_PUBLIC_ and verify each is truly public
If leaked: rotate the credential immediately, it's already compromised
Safe for NEXT_PUBLIC_: analytics IDs, public API URLs, feature flags, app name
Server-side only env vars are never sent to browser - use them for secrets
Use 'server-only' package to prevent accidental import of server modules in client components

symptoms: don't fetch in useEffect for initial data
avoid client-side data fetching when server works
root_cause: Fetching data in useEffect creates waterfalls, shows loading spinners, and hurts SEO
fix: Use Server Components for initial data: async function Page() { const data = await getData(); return <List data={data} /> }
useEffect fetching is appropriate ONLY for: user-initiated actions, real-time updates, post-mount data
Server-fetched data is available immediately (no loading state), included in HTML (SEO-friendly)
Anti-pattern: 'use client' → useEffect → fetch('/api/data') → setState → render
Better pattern: Server Component fetches → passes data as props → Client Component handles interactivity
Exception: infinite scroll, search-as-you-type, polling - these legitimately need client-side fetching

root_cause: Suppressing TypeScript errors removes the safety net and leads to runtime crashes
fix: Never use 'any' as a shortcut - use 'unknown' and narrow with type guards
Replace // @ts-ignore with // @ts-expect-error (fails if error is fixed, keeping code clean)
If you can't type something: use generic constraints, conditional types, or utility types
Don't set strict: false to fix errors - fix the actual types instead
For third-party untyped libs: create declaration file in types/library-name.d.ts
Escape hatch order: unknown > type assertion > @ts-expect-error > never use @ts-ignore or any

symptoms: don't put everything in use client
root_cause: Making entire pages client components defeats the purpose of Server Components and bloats the bundle
fix: Keep page.tsx as Server Component - only wrap interactive parts in client components
Bad: 'use client' at top of page that mostly displays static content
Good: Server Component page with small 'use client' interactive widgets (buttons, forms, toggles)
Each 'use client' boundary includes ALL its children in the client bundle
Push client boundaries to leaf components: <LikeButton /> not <EntirePostCard />
Context providers should wrap minimal subtrees, not the entire app

root_cause: Committed secrets remain in git history even after deletion and can be extracted by anyone with repo access
fix: Add .env* to .gitignore BEFORE first commit (except .env.example with placeholder values)
If already committed: rotate ALL credentials immediately, use git-filter-repo to remove from history
Use .env.example with dummy values as documentation: DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:password@localhost/db
For CI/CD: use GitHub Secrets or Vercel Environment Variables, never hardcode
Scanning: enable GitHub secret scanning and use tools like trufflehog or gitleaks
Never hardcode credentials in source code, even temporarily for testing

root_cause: Consistent naming conventions improve code navigation and reduce confusion
fix: Route files: page.tsx, layout.tsx, loading.tsx, error.tsx, not-found.tsx (Next.js conventions)
Components: PascalCase files matching component name: UserProfile.tsx exports UserProfile
Utilities/libs: camelCase: formatDate.ts, useDebounce.ts, validateEmail.ts
Types: PascalCase with descriptive suffix: UserProfile.types.ts or inline in component
API routes: lowercase with hyphens for URL segments: app/api/user-profiles/route.ts
Private folders: prefix with underscore _components/ or parentheses (group)/ for route groups

root_cause: Consistent import ordering improves readability and reduces merge conflicts
fix: Order: 1) React/Next.js 2) External packages 3) Internal aliases (@/) 4) Relative imports 5) Types
Use path aliases: import { Button } from '@/components/ui/button' (not '../../../components')
Separate type imports: import type { User } from '@/types' (tree-shakes better)
Barrel exports for component folders: components/ui/index.ts re-exports all UI components
Enforce with ESLint: eslint-plugin-import with import/order rule
Auto-sort with Prettier: @trivago/prettier-plugin-sort-imports

root_cause: Multi-tenant architecture for SaaS applications with proper data isolation
fix: Use subdomain routing: middleware.ts extracts tenant from hostname
Database isolation options: 1) Row-level (tenantId column) 2) Schema-per-tenant 3) DB-per-tenant
Row-level (recommended for most): add tenantId to every table, filter in every query
Prisma middleware for automatic filtering: prisma.$use(async (params, next) => { params.args.where = { ...params.args.where, tenantId } })
Store current tenant in AsyncLocalStorage or request context for easy access
RLS with Supabase: CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON table USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.tenant_id'))

root_cause: Event-driven architecture decouples components and enables scalable async processing
fix: Emit events on state changes: await inngest.send({ name: 'order/placed', data: order })
Separate read and write models (CQRS): writes go to main DB, reads from denormalized cache/view
Event handlers process asynchronously: order/placed → send-confirmation, update-inventory, notify-admin
Use Inngest for orchestration: multi-step workflows with automatic retries per step
Store events for audit trail: event_log table with eventType, payload, timestamp, userId
Benefits: loose coupling, better scalability, complete audit history, easy to add new handlers

root_cause: Tiered rate limiting protects your API while allowing fair usage based on subscription plan
fix: Define tiers: { free: '10/min', pro: '100/min', enterprise: '1000/min' }
Identify user: API key from header or session userId
Look up plan: const plan = await redis.get(`plan:${userId}`) || 'free'
Apply limit: const ratelimit = new Ratelimit({ redis, limiter: Ratelimit.slidingWindow(limits[plan], '1m') })
Return headers: X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset
Implement in middleware for global coverage or per-route for granular control

symptoms: when to use what for real-time
root_cause: Choose the right real-time technology based on requirements and infrastructure
fix: Supabase Realtime: best for DB-synchronized state (chat, collaborative editing, live feeds)
Server-Sent Events (SSE): best for server-to-client one-way updates (notifications, progress)
Polling with Redis: simplest, works everywhere, good for infrequent updates (<1/min)
Supabase Broadcast: best for ephemeral events (typing indicators, cursor position, game state)
Inngest + webhook: best for long-running process updates (job progress, build status)
Don't use WebSocket on Vercel serverless - no persistent connections; use Supabase/SSE instead

root_cause: Reliable background job processing that handles failures gracefully in serverless
fix: Receive trigger (API/webhook) → Validate → Acknowledge (200) → Queue for processing
Queue options: Inngest (complex workflows), QStash (simple HTTP callbacks), Redis list (basic FIFO)
Each job should be idempotent: running twice with same input produces same result
Dedup with Redis: check dedup key before processing, set on completion
Track job state: pending → processing → completed/failed (store in Redis hash or DB)
Dead letter handling: after max retries, move to DLQ, alert team, allow manual replay

root_cause: Complete Stripe Checkout integration for one-time payments or subscriptions
fix: Install: npm install stripe @stripe/stripe-js
Server: const stripe = new Stripe(process.env.STRIPE_SECRET_KEY!)
Create checkout session: app/api/checkout/route.ts → stripe.checkout.sessions.create({ mode: 'subscription', line_items: [{ price: priceId, quantity: 1 }], success_url, cancel_url })
Client redirect: const { url } = await response.json(); window.location.href = url
Webhook handler: app/api/webhooks/stripe/route.ts → verify signature with stripe.webhooks.constructEvent(body, sig, webhookSecret)
Handle events: checkout.session.completed → provision access, invoice.payment_failed → notify user
Set STRIPE_SECRET_KEY, STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET, NEXT_PUBLIC_STRIPE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY

root_cause: Complete email sending setup with Resend and React Email templates
fix: Install: npm install resend @react-email/components
Create client: import { Resend } from 'resend'; const resend = new Resend(process.env.RESEND_API_KEY)
Create template: components/emails/WelcomeEmail.tsx using @react-email/components (Html, Head, Body, Text, Link)
Send: await resend.emails.send({ from: 'App <noreply@yourdomain.com>', to: user.email, subject: 'Welcome!', react: WelcomeEmail({ name: user.name }) })
For reliable delivery: queue via Inngest with retry: inngest.send({ name: 'email/send', data: { template: 'welcome', to, vars } })
Set RESEND_API_KEY in Vercel env vars, verify domain in Resend dashboard

root_cause: Complete search implementation with debounced input, API route, and results display
fix: Client: useSearchParams + useDebouncedValue for URL-synced search input
API route: app/api/search/route.ts accepts ?q=query parameter
For PostgreSQL/Neon: use to_tsvector + plainto_tsquery for full-text search
For SQLite/Turso: use FTS5 virtual table for full-text search
For vector search: generate embedding → query pgvector with cosine similarity
Cache results in Redis with short TTL: redis.set(`search:${hash(query)}`, results, { ex: 60 })
Return highlighted snippets and metadata for rich search results UI

root_cause: Complete pagination implementation with cursor-based approach for large datasets
fix: Cursor-based (recommended): SELECT * FROM posts WHERE id < cursor ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 20
With Prisma: prisma.post.findMany({ take: 20, skip: 1, cursor: { id: lastId }, orderBy: { id: 'desc' } })
API response shape: { items: Post[], nextCursor: string | null, hasMore: boolean }
Client infinite scroll: use IntersectionObserver to detect when user reaches bottom
Or use TanStack Query useInfiniteQuery for built-in pagination state management
Add loading indicator at bottom, disable when hasMore is false
Cache pages in Redis: redis.set(`posts:page:${cursor}`, page, { ex: 300 })

symptoms: upload to Vercel Blob with progress
root_cause: Complete file upload implementation with preview, validation, and progress tracking
fix: Client form: <input type='file' accept='image/*' onChange={handleFile} /> with drag-drop zone
Preview: const preview = URL.createObjectURL(file); display in
Validate: check file.size (< 4MB), file.type (image/*) before upload
Upload to Vercel Blob: const blob = await upload(file.name, file, { access: 'public', handleUploadUrl: '/api/upload' })
Server handler: app/api/upload/route.ts with handleUpload({ onBeforeGenerateToken: validate })
Store blob.url in database associated with user/entity
For local dev: save to filesystem, serve via /api/files/[...path] route

root_cause: Essential checks before deploying a Next.js application to production on Vercel
fix: 1. All env vars set in Vercel (Production + Preview environments)
2. Database migrations applied to production: npx prisma migrate deploy
3. Build succeeds locally: npm run build (no type errors, no lint errors)
4. Security headers configured in next.config.js or middleware
5. Error boundaries in place: error.tsx, global-error.tsx, not-found.tsx
6. Analytics/monitoring setup: Sentry, Vercel Analytics, Speed Insights
7. Images use next/image, fonts use next/font, metadata configured
8. Rate limiting on public API routes, CORS configured
9. Database connection pooling enabled, indexes on query columns
10. .env.local NOT committed, secrets rotated if ever exposed

root_cause: Security checklist for Next.js applications before public launch
fix: 1. No secrets in NEXT_PUBLIC_ variables or client bundle
2. All user inputs validated with Zod (forms, URL params, API bodies)
3. SQL injection prevented (using Prisma/parameterized queries, never string concatenation)
4. XSS prevented: React auto-escapes, avoid dangerouslySetInnerHTML, sanitize markdown
5. CSRF protection: use SameSite cookies, verify Origin header in mutations
6. Authentication on all protected routes (middleware + server-side checks)
7. Rate limiting on auth endpoints (login, register, password reset)
8. Security headers: CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options
9. Dependencies updated: npm audit, no known vulnerabilities
10. Webhook endpoints verify signatures (Stripe, QStash, etc.)

root_cause: Performance optimization checklist for achieving good Core Web Vitals scores
fix: 1. LCP: Hero image uses next/image with priority prop, above-fold content server-rendered
2. CLS: All images have width/height or aspect-ratio, fonts use next/font (no FOUT)
3. INP: Event handlers are fast (<200ms), heavy work deferred with startTransition
4. Bundle: No unnecessary 'use client', dynamic imports for heavy components
5. Data: Parallel fetching with Promise.all, no sequential waterfalls
6. Caching: ISR for semi-static pages, Redis for computed data, proper cache headers
7. Database: Indexes on filtered columns, connection pooling, select only needed fields
8. Third-party: Scripts loaded with next/script strategy='lazyOnload'
9. Region: Vercel functions in same region as database
10. Verify with Lighthouse, Vercel Speed Insights, and real user monitoring

root_cause: Complete AI chat implementation with streaming responses using Vercel AI SDK
fix: Install: npm install ai openai

API route: app/api/chat/route.ts: import { openai } from '@ai-sdk/openai'; import { streamText } from 'ai'; export async function POST(req) { const { messages } = await req.json(); const result = streamText({ model: openai('gpt-4o'), messages }); return result.toDataStreamResponse() }
Client: import { useChat } from 'ai/react'; const { messages, input, handleInputChange, handleSubmit, isLoading } = useChat()

Render: messages.map(m =>
{m.role}: {m.content}
)
Set maxDuration: export const maxDuration = 60 (streaming needs longer timeout)
Set OPENAI_API_KEY in environment variables

root_cause: Complete RAG pipeline: embed query, search vectors, augment LLM prompt with context
fix: 1. Generate query embedding: const embedding = await openai.embeddings.create({ model: 'text-embedding-3-small', input: query })
2. Search vectors in Neon: SELECT * FROM documents ORDER BY embedding <=> $1::vector LIMIT 5

3. Build augmented prompt: const context = results.map(r => r.content).join('
'); const prompt = `Context:
${context}

Question: ${query}`
4. Stream LLM response: streamText({ model: openai('gpt-4o'), messages: [{ role: 'user', content: prompt }] })
5. Cache embeddings in Redis to avoid recomputing: redis.set(`embed:${hash}`, embedding, { ex: 86400 })
6. Cache retrieval results for identical queries: redis.set(`rag:${hash}`, results, { ex: 3600 })

root_cause: Well-designed prompts produce more consistent and useful AI responses
fix: Use system message for consistent behavior: { role: 'system', content: 'You are a helpful assistant that...' }
Be specific about output format: 'Respond in JSON with fields: summary, steps, confidence'
Use Zod schema with AI SDK: const result = await generateObject({ model, schema: ResponseSchema, prompt })
Include examples (few-shot): show 2-3 input/output examples in system prompt
Set temperature based on use case: 0 for factual/code, 0.7 for creative, 1.0 for brainstorming
Limit response length with maxTokens to control costs and response time

root_cause: Balanced testing strategy that maximizes confidence with minimal maintenance burden
fix: Unit tests: pure functions, utilities, validation schemas (fast, many) - use Vitest
Integration tests: server actions, API routes with real DB (medium speed, moderate count) - use Vitest
E2E tests: critical user flows (login, checkout, CRUD) - use Playwright
Don't test implementation details - test behavior and outcomes
Mock external services (Stripe, email, AI) but use real DB in integration tests
Minimum coverage: all server actions, all API routes, critical UI flows

root_cause: Need to test code that uses Prisma without hitting a real database
fix: Install: npm i -D vitest-mock-extended
Create mock: import { mockDeep } from 'vitest-mock-extended'; import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client'; const prisma = mockDeep<PrismaClient>()
Mock module: vi.mock('@/lib/db', () => ({ prisma: mockDeep<PrismaClient>() }))
Setup return values: prisma.user.findMany.mockResolvedValue([{ id: '1', name: 'Test' }])
Assert calls: expect(prisma.user.create).toHaveBeenCalledWith({ data: expect.objectContaining({ email }) })
Alternative: use test database with prisma migrate reset between test suites

symptoms: test Next.js server actions with Vitest
root_cause: Need to test Server Actions that interact with database and external services
fix: Import server action directly: import { createPost } from '@/app/actions'
Mock dependencies: vi.mock('@/lib/db'); vi.mock('next/cache', () => ({ revalidatePath: vi.fn() }))
Test success: const result = await createPost(formData); expect(result.success).toBe(true)
Test validation failure: const result = await createPost(invalidData); expect(result.error).toBeDefined()
Test with FormData: const fd = new FormData(); fd.set('title', 'Test'); await createPost(fd)
Verify side effects: expect(revalidatePath).toHaveBeenCalledWith('/posts')

root_cause: Need to test complete user flows including authentication and page interactions
fix: Install: npm i -D @playwright/test && npx playwright install
Config: playwright.config.ts with webServer: { command: 'npm run dev', port: 3000 }
Test login flow: await page.goto('/login'); await page.fill('[name=email]', 'test@example.com'); await page.click('button[type=submit]'); await expect(page).toHaveURL('/dashboard')
Use fixtures for auth state: test.use({ storageState: 'auth.json' }) to skip login in most tests
Test API: const response = await request.post('/api/posts', { data: { title: 'Test' } }); expect(response.ok()).toBeTruthy()
Run: npx playwright test --run (single run, not watch mode for CI)

root_cause: Complete internationalization setup with locale routing and translations
fix: Install: npm install next-intl
Create messages: messages/en.json and messages/id.json with translation keys
Middleware: detect locale from Accept-Language header or cookie, redirect to /en/... or /id/...
Route structure: app/[locale]/page.tsx with generateStaticParams for all locales
Layout: wrap with NextIntlClientProvider: <NextIntlClientProvider messages={messages}>

Use in components: import { useTranslations } from 'next-intl'; const t = useTranslations('HomePage'); return

{t('title')}
Server components: import { getTranslations } from 'next-intl/server'

root_cause: Proper internationalization handles more than just text translation
fix: Use ICU message format for plurals: {count, plural, one {# item} other {# items}}
Format dates/numbers with Intl API: new Intl.DateTimeFormat(locale).format(date)
Store user locale preference in database/cookie, not just URL
Use locale-aware sorting: array.sort((a, b) => a.localeCompare(b, locale))
Handle RTL languages: add dir='rtl' to html element based on locale
Keep translation keys semantic (not the English text): 'nav.home' not 'Home'

root_cause: Structured logging enables efficient debugging and alerting in serverless environments
fix: Use structured JSON logs: console.log(JSON.stringify({ level: 'error', message, context, timestamp }))
Include correlation IDs: pass requestId through all log entries for request tracing
Log levels: error (action needed), warn (unusual), info (business events), debug (dev only)
In production: use Vercel Log Drains to forward to Datadog/Axiom/Betterstack
Never log sensitive data: PII, tokens, passwords, full request bodies with secrets
Use pino for structured logging: npm install pino; logger.info({ userId, action }, 'User created')

root_cause: Need comprehensive error tracking that captures both client and server errors
fix: Install: npx @sentry/wizard@latest -i nextjs (auto-configures everything)
Manual: npm install @sentry/nextjs; create sentry.client.config.ts, sentry.server.config.ts, sentry.edge.config.ts
Wrap next.config: const { withSentryConfig } = require('@sentry/nextjs'); module.exports = withSentryConfig(nextConfig)
Capture manually: import * as Sentry from '@sentry/nextjs'; Sentry.captureException(error)
Add context: Sentry.setUser({ id: userId, email }); Sentry.setTag('feature', 'checkout')
Custom error boundary: error.tsx can report to Sentry before showing fallback UI
Set SENTRY_DSN, SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN, SENTRY_ORG, SENTRY_PROJECT in Vercel env vars

root_cause: Effective alerting prevents alert fatigue while catching real issues
fix: Alert on: error rate spike (>1% of requests), function timeouts, payment failures, auth failures
Log only: individual 404s, validation errors, rate-limited requests, slow queries
Use thresholds: alert if error count > 10 in 5 minutes, not on every single error
Critical alerts (page immediately): payment processing down, database unreachable, auth system broken
Warning alerts (check within hours): elevated error rate, approaching rate limits, disk usage high
Review and tune alerts monthly - remove noisy alerts, add for recent incidents

root_cause: Accessible applications work for all users including those using assistive technology
fix: Semantic HTML: use <button>, <nav>, <main>, <article> instead of <div> for everything
Images: always provide alt text; decorative images use alt='' (empty, not missing)
Forms: every input needs a <label htmlFor={id}>, error messages linked with aria-describedby
Focus management: after navigation/modal open, move focus appropriately
Color contrast: minimum 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text (use Tailwind's built-in accessible colors)
Keyboard navigation: all interactive elements reachable via Tab, Escape closes modals
Test with screen reader (VoiceOver/NVDA) and keyboard-only navigation

root_cause: Need an accessible modal that traps focus, handles Escape key, and announces to screen readers
fix: Use native <dialog> element or Radix UI Dialog for built-in a11y
Required attributes: role='dialog', aria-modal='true', aria-labelledby='title-id'
Focus trap: on open, focus first focusable element; Tab cycles within modal only
Escape key: close modal on Escape press, return focus to trigger element
Backdrop: clicking outside closes modal; add aria-hidden='true' to content behind modal
With Radix: import { Dialog, DialogContent, DialogTitle } from '@radix-ui/react-dialog' (handles all a11y automatically)
Tailwind animation: animate-in fade-in zoom-in-95 for smooth enter, animate-out for exit

root_cause: Utility types reduce boilerplate and enforce constraints at the type level
fix: Partial<T>: make all fields optional (for update operations)
Required<T>: make all fields required (for creation operations)
Pick<T, 'field1' | 'field2'>: extract subset of fields (for API responses)
Omit<T, 'password' | 'secret'>: remove sensitive fields before sending to client
Record<string, T>: typed dictionary/map objects
Custom: type DeepPartial<T> = { [P in keyof T]?: T[P] extends object ? DeepPartial<T[P]> : T[P] }
Branded types: type UserId = string & { __brand: 'UserId' } - prevents mixing IDs accidentally

root_cause: Discriminated unions enable exhaustive type checking for state machines and variants
fix: Define: type Result<T> = { status: 'success'; data: T } | { status: 'error'; error: string } | { status: 'loading' }
Use with switch: switch (result.status) { case 'success': result.data; case 'error': result.error } // TypeScript narrows automatically
For API states: type AsyncState<T> = { state: 'idle' } | { state: 'loading' } | { state: 'success'; data: T } | { state: 'error'; error: Error }
Exhaustive check helper: function assertNever(x: never): never { throw new Error('Unexpected: ' + x) }
Use in reducers: each action type narrows payload type automatically
React pattern: render different UI for each discriminant without type assertions

root_cause: Generate dynamic OpenGraph images for rich social media previews
fix: Create app/opengraph-image.tsx (or per-route: app/blog/[slug]/opengraph-image.tsx)

Use ImageResponse from next/og: export default async function Image({ params }) { return new ImageResponse(
Title
, { width: 1200, height: 630 }) }
Or generate per-page: export async function generateMetadata({ params }) { return { openGraph: { images: [`/api/og?title=${title}`] } } }
API route approach: app/api/og/route.tsx for more flexibility with query params
Use @vercel/og for edge-compatible image generation with JSX templating
Test with social media debuggers: Twitter Card Validator, Facebook Sharing Debugger

root_cause: Need dynamic sitemap that includes all pages for search engine crawling
fix: Create app/sitemap.ts: import { MetadataRoute } from 'next'; export default async function sitemap(): Promise<MetadataRoute.Sitemap> { const posts = await prisma.post.findMany({ select: { slug: true, updatedAt: true } }); return [{ url: 'https://example.com', lastModified: new Date() }, ...posts.map(p => ({ url: `https://example.com/blog/${p.slug}`, lastModified: p.updatedAt }))] }
For large sites: use app/sitemap/[id]/route.ts for paginated sitemaps
Add robots.txt: create app/robots.ts: export default function robots(): MetadataRoute.Robots { return { rules: { userAgent: '*', allow: '/' }, sitemap: 'https://example.com/sitemap.xml' } }
Vercel automatically serves at /sitemap.xml and /robots.txt

root_cause: Parallel data fetching eliminates sequential request waterfalls and reduces page load time
fix: Use Promise.all for independent data: const [posts, categories, user] = await Promise.all([getPosts(), getCategories(), getUser()])
In Server Components: each component fetches independently, React deduplicates matching requests
Preload pattern: export const preload = (id: string) => { void getItem(id) }; // call in parent, data ready in child
React cache() for deduplication: const getUser = cache(async (id) => prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { id } }))
Never fetch sequentially when data is independent: BAD: const a = await getA(); const b = await getB()
Suspense boundaries let fast components render while slow ones stream in

root_cause: Route groups organize code and apply layouts to subsets of routes without affecting URL path
fix: Create with parentheses: app/(marketing)/about/page.tsx → URL is /about (no /marketing)
Use for shared layouts: app/(auth)/login/page.tsx and app/(auth)/register/page.tsx share (auth)/layout.tsx
Multiple root layouts: app/(marketing)/layout.tsx and app/(app)/layout.tsx for completely different UIs
Organize by feature: app/(dashboard)/settings/, app/(dashboard)/profile/ share dashboard layout
Don't nest route groups: keep flat for clarity
Combine with middleware: matcher can target routes within a group

root_cause: SSE provides real-time server-to-client updates without WebSocket complexity on Vercel
fix: Create SSE endpoint: app/api/events/route.ts

Return streaming response: return new Response(new ReadableStream({ start(controller) { const encoder = new TextEncoder(); const send = (data: any) => controller.enqueue(encoder.encode(`data: ${JSON.stringify(data)}\n\n`)); // Keep connection alive, send events... } }), { headers: { 'Content-Type': 'text/event-stream', 'Cache-Control': 'no-cache', Connection: 'keep-alive' } })
Client: const eventSource = new EventSource('/api/events'); eventSource.onmessage = (e) => { const data = JSON.parse(e.data); setState(data) }
Close on unmount: useEffect cleanup: () => eventSource.close()
Limitation on Vercel: function timeout applies (max 60s Pro), reconnect on disconnect
Alternative for longer: use Supabase Realtime for persistent connections

root_cause: Middleware runs on every matched request - keep it fast and focused
fix: Keep middleware fast: <10ms execution, no heavy computation or database queries
Good for: auth checks (JWT verify), redirects, A/B testing, locale detection, rate limiting (with Redis)
Bad for: complex business logic, database queries, file processing
Use matcher to skip static assets: export const config = { matcher: ['/((?!_next/static|_next/image|favicon.ico).*)'] }
Middleware runs on Edge Runtime: only use edge-compatible packages
Chain concerns in order: rate limit → auth → locale → rewrite
Return NextResponse.next() to continue, NextResponse.redirect() to redirect, NextResponse.rewrite() for URL masking

root_cause: Soft delete preserves data for audit trails and undo functionality
fix: Add field: deletedAt DateTime? to models that need soft delete

Middleware approach: prisma.$use(async (params, next) => { if (params.action === 'delete') { params.action = 'update'; params.args.data = { deletedAt: new Date() } } if (['findMany','findFirst'].includes(params.action)) { params.args.where = { ...params.args.where, deletedAt: null } } return next(params) })
Or explicit: prisma.post.findMany({ where: { deletedAt: null } })
Restore: await prisma.post.update({ where: { id }, data: { deletedAt: null } })
Hard delete: await prisma.post.delete({ where: { id } }) (bypass middleware if needed)
Add index on deletedAt for efficient filtering: @@index([deletedAt])

root_cause: Audit logging tracks all data changes for compliance, debugging, and undo features
fix: Create AuditLog model: model AuditLog { id String @id @default(cuid()) action String (CREATE/UPDATE/DELETE) tableName String recordId String userId String changes Json createdAt DateTime @default(now()) }

Middleware: prisma.$use(async (params, next) => { const result = await next(params); if (['create','update','delete'].includes(params.action)) { await prisma.auditLog.create({ data: { action: params.action, tableName: params.model, recordId: result.id, userId: getCurrentUserId(), changes: params.args.data } }) } return result })
Query history: prisma.auditLog.findMany({ where: { tableName: 'Post', recordId: postId }, orderBy: { createdAt: 'desc' } })
Store diff for updates: compare before/after to only log changed fields
For high-traffic: buffer audit logs in Redis, flush to DB periodically

root_cause: Optimistic locking prevents lost updates when multiple users edit the same record
fix: Add version field: version Int @default(0)
Update with version check: const updated = await prisma.post.updateMany({ where: { id, version: currentVersion }, data: { ...changes, version: { increment: 1 } } })
Check result: if (updated.count === 0) throw new Error('Conflict: record was modified by another user')
Client flow: fetch record with version → user edits → submit with version → if conflict, show latest and ask user to merge
Return 409 Conflict status to client with latest data for resolution
Alternative: use updatedAt timestamp instead of version number for simpler cases

symptoms: Redis sorted set for activity feed
root_cause: Sorted sets enable efficient time-ordered feeds with O(log N) insertion and O(log N + M) range queries
fix: Add to feed: await redis.zadd(`feed:${userId}`, { score: Date.now(), member: JSON.stringify({ type: 'post', id: postId, actorId }) })
Get latest: const items = await redis.zrange(`feed:${userId}`, 0, 19, { rev: true }) // newest 20
Paginate with cursor: await redis.zrangebyscore(`feed:${userId}`, '-inf', lastScore, { limit: { offset: 0, count: 20 } })
Trim old entries: await redis.zremrangebyrank(`feed:${userId}`, 0, -101) // keep only last 100
Fan-out on write: when user posts, add to all followers' feeds
For high-traffic: fan-out on read (build feed at query time) for users with many followers

root_cause: Redis GEO commands enable finding nearby items within a radius efficiently
fix: Add locations: await redis.geoadd('restaurants', { longitude: -73.935242, latitude: 40.730610, member: 'restaurant:123' })
Find nearby: const results = await redis.georadius('restaurants', longitude, latitude, 5, 'km', { withDist: true, count: 10, sort: 'ASC' })
Get distance: await redis.geodist('restaurants', 'restaurant:123', 'restaurant:456', 'km')
Search by member: await redis.georadiusbymember('restaurants', 'restaurant:123', 10, 'km')
Store metadata separately: use restaurant:123 as key for hash with name, rating, etc.
Combine with sorted sets: rank by distance + rating for relevance ordering

root_cause: Track real-time metrics using Redis sorted sets as sliding windows
fix: Track event: await redis.zadd(`metrics:${event}:${windowKey}`, { score: Date.now(), member: `${Date.now()}-${Math.random()}` })
Count in window: await redis.zcount(`metrics:pageview:${hour}`, Date.now() - 60000, '+inf') // last minute
Cleanup old: await redis.zremrangebyscore(`metrics:pageview:${hour}`, '-inf', Date.now() - 3600000)
Multiple windows: track per-minute, per-hour, per-day in separate keys
Aggregate: pipeline multiple zcount calls for dashboard data
Auto-expire keys: set TTL matching the window: await redis.expire(key, 3600)

root_cause: Database triggers automate common patterns like updating timestamps and maintaining derived data
fix: Auto-update updated_at: CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION update_modified_column() RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$ BEGIN NEW.updated_at = NOW(); RETURN NEW; END $$ LANGUAGE plpgsql; CREATE TRIGGER update_timestamp BEFORE UPDATE ON posts FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION update_modified_column()

Auto-create profile on user signup: CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION create_profile() RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$ BEGIN INSERT INTO profiles (id, email) VALUES (NEW.id, NEW.email); RETURN NEW; END $$ LANGUAGE plpgsql; CREATE TRIGGER on_auth_user_created AFTER INSERT ON auth.users FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION create_profile()
Maintain counters: trigger that increments/decrements comment_count on posts table when comments inserted/deleted
Manage in Supabase Dashboard > Database > Triggers or via SQL migrations
Triggers run inside the transaction - keep them fast to avoid blocking

root_cause: Need structured migration workflow for Supabase database schema changes
fix: Install Supabase CLI: npm install -D supabase
Init: npx supabase init (creates supabase/ directory with config)
Link to project: npx supabase link --project-ref your-project-ref
Create migration: npx supabase migration new add_comments_table
Edit migration SQL: supabase/migrations/[timestamp]_add_comments_table.sql
Apply locally: npx supabase db reset (applies all migrations to local DB)
Deploy to production: npx supabase db push (applies pending migrations to remote)
Generate from diff: npx supabase db diff --use-migra -f migration_name (auto-generates SQL)

root_cause: Tailwind Typography plugin styles rendered markdown/HTML content beautifully with minimal effort
fix: Install: npm install @tailwindcss/typography, add to plugins in tailwind.config
Use: <article className='prose prose-lg dark:prose-invert max-w-none'>{renderedMarkdown}</article>
Customize: prose-headings:text-brand-500 prose-a:text-blue-600 prose-code:bg-gray-100
Override max-width: max-w-none for full-width content, or prose-sm/prose-lg for size
For blog posts: prose dark:prose-invert handles light/dark mode automatically
Extend in config: typography: { DEFAULT: { css: { color: 'var(--text)' } } }

root_cause: CSS Grid with Tailwind enables complex responsive layouts without custom CSS
fix: Auto-fit responsive: className='grid grid-cols-[repeat(auto-fit,minmax(250px,1fr))] gap-4'
Dashboard layout: className='grid grid-cols-[250px_1fr] grid-rows-[64px_1fr] h-screen'
Card grid: className='grid grid-cols-1 sm:grid-cols-2 lg:grid-cols-3 xl:grid-cols-4 gap-6'
Span columns: className='col-span-2' or 'col-span-full' for full-width items
Named areas (in CSS): grid-template-areas with Tailwind arbitrary values
Masonry (vertical): use CSS columns instead: className='columns-2 md:columns-3 gap-4 space-y-4'

root_cause: Need consistent, accessible form styling with Tailwind
fix: Install: npm install @tailwindcss/forms, add to plugins in tailwind.config
Plugin provides base styles for inputs, selects, checkboxes, radio buttons
Text input: className='rounded-md border-gray-300 shadow-sm focus:border-blue-500 focus:ring-blue-500'
Select: className='rounded-md border-gray-300 focus:border-blue-500 focus:ring-blue-500'
Checkbox: className='rounded border-gray-300 text-blue-600 focus:ring-blue-500'
Error state: className='border-red-500 focus:border-red-500 focus:ring-red-500'
Disabled: className='disabled:bg-gray-100 disabled:cursor-not-allowed opacity-50'

root_cause: Automated CI/CD catches issues before deployment and ensures consistent quality
fix: Lint + Type check on every PR: npm run lint && tsc --noEmit
Run tests on PR: npm run test (Vitest unit + integration tests)
Preview deployments: Vercel automatically creates preview for every PR
Production deploy: only from main branch, after PR merge
GitHub Actions workflow: on: pull_request → lint, typecheck, test, build
Post-deploy: run smoke tests against preview URL, verify critical flows
Protect main branch: require status checks (CI pass) before merge

root_cause: Need automated CI that runs on every pull request
fix: Create .github/workflows/ci.yml:

name: CI; on: pull_request; jobs: ci: runs-on: ubuntu-latest; steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - uses: actions/setup-node@v4 with: node-version: 20 cache: npm - run: npm ci - run: npm run lint - run: npx tsc --noEmit - run: npm run test -- --run - run: npm run build
Add caching for node_modules and .next/cache for faster builds
Set environment variables in GitHub Secrets for test environment
Add Playwright step for E2E: npx playwright install --with-deps && npx playwright test
Use matrix strategy for multiple Node.js versions if supporting multiple

root_cause: Preview deployments enable testing changes in production-like environment before merge
fix: Vercel auto-creates preview deployment for every git push to non-main branches
Each preview has unique URL: your-project-git-branch-name-username.vercel.app
Preview env vars: set separately from production in Vercel Dashboard
Use Neon branching: auto-create database branch per preview deployment
Test with preview: share URL with reviewers, run E2E tests against it
Preview comments: Vercel bot comments PR with deployment link automatically
Protect production: never deploy directly to production without preview + review

root_cause: Proper input handling prevents injection attacks and data corruption
fix: React auto-escapes JSX expressions: {userInput} is safe by default
NEVER use dangerouslySetInnerHTML with user content without sanitization
If rendering HTML: use DOMPurify: import DOMPurify from 'dompurify'; const clean = DOMPurify.sanitize(html)
Validate all inputs server-side with Zod (client validation is UX only, not security)
Sanitize before database storage, not just before display
URL parameters: validate expected format, reject unexpected characters
File uploads: validate MIME type server-side (don't trust client content-type header)

root_cause: Need CSRF protection for mutation endpoints that use cookie-based authentication
fix: Server Actions have built-in CSRF protection (Origin header check) - use them when possible
For API routes: verify Origin or Referer header matches your domain
Pattern: export async function POST(req) { const origin = req.headers.get('origin'); if (origin !== process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL) return Response.json({ error: 'CSRF' }, { status: 403 }) }
SameSite cookies provide additional protection: set SameSite: 'lax' on session cookies
For extra security: implement double-submit cookie pattern or synchronizer token
Custom header approach: require X-Requested-With header (browsers don't send it cross-origin)

root_cause: Secure dependencies prevent supply chain attacks which are increasingly common
fix: Run npm audit regularly and fix critical/high vulnerabilities
Use lockfile (package-lock.json) and commit it - ensures reproducible installs
Pin exact versions for critical dependencies: "stripe": "14.0.0" not "^14.0.0"
Enable GitHub Dependabot for automated security PRs
Review new dependencies before installing: check npm stats, GitHub activity, maintainers
Use npm provenance when available to verify package origin
Minimize dependency count: fewer deps = smaller attack surface

root_cause: Saga pattern handles distributed transactions with compensation steps on failure
fix: Define saga function: inngest.createFunction({ id: 'place-order' }, { event: 'order/place' }, async ({ event, step }) => {
const payment = await step.run('charge-payment', () => chargeStripe(event.data.amount))
const inventory = await step.run('reserve-inventory', () => reserveItems(event.data.items))
try { await step.run('ship-order', () => createShipment(event.data)) }
catch (e) { // Compensate: undo previous steps
await step.run('refund-payment', () => refundStripe(payment.chargeId))
await step.run('release-inventory', () => releaseItems(inventory.reservationId))
throw e } })
Each step is independently retried; compensation only runs if a later step fails permanently
Store step results for compensation: the payment.chargeId is available for refund

root_cause: Batch publishing sends multiple messages in a single API call for efficiency
fix: Batch publish: await qstash.batchJSON([{ destination: '/api/process', body: { id: 1 } }, { destination: '/api/process', body: { id: 2 } }, { destination: '/api/notify', body: { message: 'done' } }])
Up to 100 messages per batch call
Each message can have different destination, delay, headers
Use for fan-out: process list of items in parallel
Combine with delay for staggered execution: { delay: index * 5 } // 5s apart
Monitor in Upstash Console: see delivery status for each message in batch

root_cause: Need to replay or reprocess events that failed during initial processing
fix: Inngest dashboard: manually replay any failed function execution from the UI
Programmatic replay: await inngest.send({ name: 'original/event', data: originalPayload }) // re-triggers all handlers
Batch replay from database: query failed events from audit log, re-send each
Idempotency is key: ensure replayed events don't create duplicates (check with Redis dedup)
Pattern: store event in DB before processing → on replay, check completion status first
Dead letter recovery: periodically scan DLQ, attempt reprocessing or escalate

root_cause: Progressive Web App features enable offline access and native-like experience
